requested_services_and_codes.requested_item_service:
Comprehensive Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) treatment program for Autism Spectrum Disorder, including initial and ongoing behavior assessment, treatment plan development, direct one-on-one ABA therapy, parent/caregiver training, and supervision of behavior technicians. Requesting 6-month authorization for a medically necessary intensive early-intervention program focused on functional communication, reduction of self-injurious and aggressive behaviors, development of adaptive living skills (toileting, feeding, dressing), social/play skills, and safety skills (elopement prevention and community safety). Services to be provided in a combination of clinic-based and home-based settings, with coordination with the member’s school IEP team.

Requested units over 6 months (approximately 26 weeks):
- Initial and ongoing behavior assessment and treatment planning (BCBA): up to 16 hours total
- Direct ABA therapy with behavior technician under BCBA supervision: average 15 hours/week (approximately 390 hours total)
- BCBA direct treatment and program modification: average 2 hours/week (approximately 52 hours total)
- BCBA supervision of technicians and team meetings: average 2 hours/week (approximately 52 hours total)
- Parent/caregiver training: 2 hours/month (approximately 12 hours total)
requested_services_and_codes.cpt_codes: 97151 (Behavior identification assessment, by physician or other qualified health care professional); 97153 (Adaptive behavior treatment by protocol, administered by technician, face-to-face with one patient); 97155 (Adaptive behavior treatment with protocol modification, administered by physician or other qualified health care professional); 97156 (Family adaptive behavior treatment guidance, administered by physician or other qualified health care professional); 97158 (Group adaptive behavior treatment by protocol, administered by physician or other qualified health care professional)

expedited_review_and_in_network_benefits.explanation_required_expedite:
Ethan is a 6-year-old boy with a formal diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder (ICD-10 F84.0), confirmed by a developmental pediatrician at age 3.5. He currently demonstrates significant deficits in functional communication, social interaction, and adaptive living skills, and he engages in frequent behaviors that pose safety concerns.

Over the past three months, Ethan’s challenging behaviors have escalated in both frequency and intensity. He has begun engaging in head-banging against hard surfaces (e.g., walls, floors, and tabletops) when denied access to preferred items or when demands are placed on him. His parents report an average of 8–10 episodes per day, some lasting up to 10 minutes despite attempts at redirection. On two occasions, his mother has taken him to urgent care due to swelling and bruising on his forehead. The urgent care provider recommended closer behavioral support and indicated concern about the risk of concussion if these behaviors continue unchecked.

Ethan also engages in elopement—attempting to bolt from the home and from public settings such as parking lots and grocery stores. His parents report at least 3 attempted elopements per week, including one incident in October 2024 where he ran into a residential street and narrowly avoided being struck by a car. His mother now avoids taking him on routine errands due to safety concerns, which is significantly impacting family functioning and Ethan’s ability to access community activities.

At school, Ethan has been placed on a modified schedule because he cannot safely remain in the classroom for a full day. The school has documented multiple instances of aggression toward staff (hitting, kicking, and scratching when presented with non-preferred tasks). Without timely ABA intervention, there is a high risk of further school exclusion, regression in skills, and increased likelihood of injury to Ethan, his peers, and caregivers.

An expedited review is medically necessary in order to initiate intensive ABA services as soon as possible. Delaying treatment by several weeks while awaiting a standard authorization decision would likely result in:
- Continued and possibly worsening self-injurious behavior, increasing the risk of head trauma and emergency department visits.
- Deterioration of the limited communication skills Ethan currently has (single-word requests and picture exchange), which may further increase behavioral outbursts due to frustration.
- Additional restrictions on his school attendance and potential loss of his current classroom placement.
- Heightened caregiver stress; both parents are already missing work to manage crises and have expressed feeling overwhelmed and fearful for Ethan’s safety.

Early intensive ABA intervention is strongly supported by clinical guidelines and evidence-based practice for children Ethan’s age with his profile of needs. Rapid initiation of services will allow us to implement a detailed behavior intervention plan (BIP), teach alternative communication strategies, and put in place structured safety protocols to reduce self-injury and elopement. Given the imminent risk of harm and the likelihood of preventable emergency care utilization, an expedited review is clinically justified and in the best interest of the member’s health and safety.
